skill.name: api-design
skill.description: Design RESTful and GraphQL APIs following best practices. Use when creating new APIs, refactoring existing endpoints, or documenting API specifications. Handles OpenAPI, REST, GraphQL, versioning.
skill.summary: Design RESTful and GraphQL APIs following best practices with comprehensive guidance on REST conventions, GraphQL schemas, OpenAPI specs, and API versioning.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: All 76 static findings are false positives. The skill is pure documentation providing API design guidance. The scanner detected backtick formatting in markdown examples, legitimate documentation URLs, and JWT authentication references - none of which constitute security risks.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Detection (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Scanner detected backticks (`) in markdown used for HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) and URL paths - not shell commands
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: All backtick usage is markdown code formatting for HTTP methods and URLs documented in the API design guide

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded URL Detection (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Scanner detected example URLs in OpenAPI spec documentation and reference links to external documentation sites
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: URLs are legitimate documentation references (swagger.io, restfulapi.net) and example API URLs - no network exfiltration risk

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detection (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Scanner detected JWT algorithm identifiers (HS256, HS512) and example JWT tokens in authentication documentation
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: JWT is standard authentication. The skill documents JWT usage, not implements weak cryptography. Example tokens are placeholders

dangerous_patterns.title: System Reconnaissance Detection (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Scanner detected HTTP method 'GET' which contains letter G - not reconnaissance activity
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: False positive triggered by HTTP method name 'GET' in documentation text

content.value_statement: This skill provides comprehensive guidance for designing RESTful and GraphQL APIs following industry best practices. It helps developers create well-structured, versioned, and documented APIs.
actual_capabilities: Design RESTful API endpoints following conventions (nouns not verbs, plural names, nested resources)
Create GraphQL schemas with queries, mutations, and proper type definitions
Generate OpenAPI 3.0 specifications for API documentation
Implement API versioning strategies (URL path, header-based)
Define authentication patterns using JWT, OAuth 2.0, and API keys
Structure error responses, pagination, filtering, and sorting patterns
limitations: Does not generate actual code implementation
Does not validate existing API implementations
Does not test or mock API endpoints
Does not provide runtime security scanning

use_cases.title: New API Development
use_cases.description: Use when starting a new API project to establish proper conventions, resource naming, and endpoint structure from the beginning

use_cases.title: API Documentation
use_cases.description: Generate OpenAPI specifications and documentation for existing or new APIs to share with frontend teams and external consumers

use_cases.title: API Review and Refactoring
use_cases.description: Review existing API designs against best practices and identify areas for improvement in naming, versioning, and structure

prompt_templates.title: Design Basic REST API
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a REST API for a [resource name] resource. Include endpoint definitions, HTTP methods, response codes, and request/response formats.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating a simple CRUD API for a single resource

prompt_templates.title: Design API with Relationships
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a REST API with these resources: [list resources] and their relationships. Include nested resources and proper URL structure.

prompt_templates.title: Create OpenAPI Specification
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an OpenAPI 3.0 specification for an API that has these endpoints: [list endpoints]. Include request/response schemas, parameters, and authentication.

prompt_templates.title: Design GraphQL Schema
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a GraphQL schema for [use case]. Include type definitions, queries, mutations, and appropriate resolvers.

output_examples.input: Design a REST API for a blog with posts and comments
output_examples.output: Endpoints: GET/POST /api/v1/posts, GET/PUT/DELETE /api/v1/posts/{id}, GET/POST /api/v1/posts/{id}/comments, Response codes: 200, 201, 204, 400, 401, 403, 404

output_examples.input: Create OpenAPI spec for user management
output_examples.output: OpenAPI 3.0 with /users endpoint, GET (list users with pagination), POST (create user), JWT Bearer auth, 200/201 responses with User schema

best_practices: Use consistent resource naming with nouns, not verbs, and plural forms
Version APIs from the start using URL path versioning (e.g., /api/v1/)
Implement proper error handling with consistent error response format including error codes and details
anti_patterns: Using verbs in endpoint names (e.g., /getUsers instead of /users)
Not versioning APIs, breaking clients when changes are needed
Returning different error formats across endpoints

faq.question: What is the difference between PUT and PATCH?
faq.answer: PUT replaces the entire resource with the provided data. PATCH updates only the specified fields. Use PUT for full replacement, PATCH for partial updates.

faq.question: When should I use GraphQL instead of REST?
faq.answer: Use GraphQL when clients need flexible data fetching, multiple resources in one request, or when the data shape varies by client. Use REST for simpler APIs with predictable endpoints.

faq.question: How do I version my API?
faq.answer: The recommended approach is URL path versioning: /api/v1/users, /api/v2/users. Header versioning is also possible but less visible to clients.

faq.question: What HTTP status codes should I use?
faq.answer: Use 200 for successful reads, 201 for created resources, 204 for successful deletions, 400 for bad input, 401 for unauthorized, 403 for forbidden, 404 for not found, 500 for server errors.

faq.question: How do I handle pagination?
faq.answer: Use query parameters like page and limit. Return pagination metadata including total count, current page, and next/previous links in the response.

faq.question: What authentication methods does this skill cover?
faq.answer: The skill covers JWT (JSON Web Tokens), OAuth 2.0, API Keys, and session-based authentication with examples for each.
